points: The line of sight is the straight line drawn from the observer's eye to the point on the object being viewed.
The angle of elevation is the angle between the line of sight and the horizontal when the object is above eye level; the angle of depression is when the object is below eye level.
Both angles of elevation and depression are measured from the horizontal line and form acute angles in right-angled triangles used for calculations.
Trigonometric ratios (sin, cos, tan) are applied to right-angled triangles formed in height and distance problems to find unknown heights or distances.
The process involves drawing a diagram, identifying the right-angled triangle, selecting the appropriate trigonometric ratio based on known and unknown sides, and solving the resulting equation.
The observer's height must be accounted for when the observer is not at ground level; it is added to or subtracted from calculated vertical distances.
For single angle problems: use tan θ = opposite/adjacent when horizontal distance and angle are known to find height; use tan θ or sin θ when height and angle are known to find distance.
For multiple angle problems: set up separate equations from different angles and solve simultaneously to find multiple unknowns such as height and horizontal distance.
Alternate interior angles property shows that angle of elevation from one point equals angle of depression from another when both observe the same object along parallel horizontal lines.
